2. Verify: grep -r '25 workflow\|28 commands\|26\)' CLAUDE.md .claude/ .claude-plugin/

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: DECISION: Updated command counts to 29 in CLAUDE.md (2 places), plugin-catalog.md (header + 3 missing entries added), and marketplace.json description. Also added beads-design, beads-quick, beads-import to plugin-catalog.md table.

title: plan-view.js redundant process.on('exit', cleanup) registration
description:
## Issue
bin/plan-view.js registers `process.on('exit', cleanup)` but all exit paths already call cleanup() explicitly. The handler fires redundantly on every exit.

## Location
bin/plan-view.js: process.on('exit') registration

## Fix
Remove the `process.on('exit', cleanup)` registration. Keep the explicit cleanup() calls before each process.exit().

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] cleanup() called exactly once per exit
- [ ] Terminal state correctly restored in all exit scenarios

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: DECISION: Removed process.on('exit', cleanup) — all exit paths (SIGINT, SIGTERM, 'q' key) already call cleanup() explicitly before process.exit(); the handler was a redundant double-call

title: plan-view.js detail panel truncates long bead descriptions (magic +20 buffer)
description:
## Issue
bin/plan-view.js renderDetailPanel uses `contentHeight + 20` as a magic number for scroll headroom. Beads with 50+ line descriptions get silently truncated.

## Location
bin/plan-view.js: renderDetailPanel function

## Fix
Remove the hard height cap from renderDetailPanel, or use a larger scroll buffer that adapts to content length.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Long bead descriptions (50+ lines) fully visible via scrolling
- [ ] No arbitrary truncation

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: FACT: Already resolved in Wave 1 — replaced contentHeight+20 with Math.max(contentHeight*4, 80) so buffer scales with terminal size

title: plan-view.js contentHeight can go negative on tiny terminals
description:
## Issue
bin/plan-view.js: `contentHeight = rows - 3` goes negative on 2-row terminals. No crash but renders only header/footer with no content visible.

## Location
bin/plan-view.js: terminal height calculation

## Fix
`const contentHeight = Math.max(0, rows - 3);`

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] No negative contentHeight on any terminal size
- [ ] No crash on tiny/minimal terminals

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: FACT: Math.max(0, rows - 3) prevents negative contentHeight; also replaced magic +20 scroll buffer with Math.max(contentHeight * 4, 80) so long descriptions scale with terminal size

title: args.find() called twice in install.js TARGET_PATH parsing (code quality)
description:
## Issue
bin/install.js:52-54 calls args.find() twice with identical predicate — redundant evaluation.

## Location
bin/install.js:52-54

## Fix
```js
const positional = args.find((a) => !a.startsWith("-"));
const TARGET_PATH = positional ? path.resolve(positional) : null;
```

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] args.find() called once
- [ ] TARGET_PATH behavior unchanged

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: FACT: Already resolved in Wave 1 via _positional extraction in bdcompound-807.9 fix — single args.find() call

title: plan-export.js --help exits with code 1 instead of 0
description:
## Issue
bin/plan-export.js usage() calls process.exit(1) for --help, inconsistent with plan-view.js which exits 0. Breaks `cmd --help && do_something` shell idioms.

## Location
bin/plan-export.js:27

## Fix
Change `process.exit(1)` to `process.exit(0)` in the usage() function.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] `bd-plan-export --help` exits with code 0
- [ ] Consistent with plan-view.js

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: DECISION: Added exitCode parameter to usage() defaulting to 1; --help callers pass 0 explicitly — cleaner than duplicating print logic

title: Bun version unpinned in CI (bun-version: latest)
description:
## Issue
.github/workflows/test-installation.yml uses `bun-version: latest` and `oven-sh/setup-bun@v1` (mutable tags). Unpinned versions can introduce unexpected breakage.

## Location
.github/workflows/test-installation.yml: Setup Bun step

## Fix
Pin to specific version: `bun-version: '1.2.5'` and pin action to commit SHA.

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] Bun version pinned to specific semver
- [ ] Action pinned to commit SHA or specific tag

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: DECISION: Pinned to bun 1.3.6 (current installed version) and setup-bun@v2; this is a build-only tool so minor version drift risk is low but pinning is good hygiene

title: execSync timeout missing in plan-export.js — hangs indefinitely on stuck bd
description:
## Issue
bin/plan-export.js bdExecFile calls have no timeout. Unlike plan-view.js (which sets timeout: 10000), a stuck bd process will block forever.

## Location
bin/plan-export.js: bdExecFile function

## Fix
Add timeout to execFileSync options: `{ encoding: 'utf8', stdio: [...], timeout: 30000 }`

## Validation Criteria
- [ ] plan-export.js exits with error if bd takes >30s

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: DECISION: Set timeout: 30000 (30s) to match plan-view.js pattern but longer since export is a one-shot operation not interactive

title: Create install-opencode.sh installer script
description:
Create a dedicated OpenCode installer script once commands/skills/agents are ported.

## Context

bdcompound-zgi was closed with decision to use manual setup (YAGNI for MVP). But now that we know OpenCode supports commands/skills/agents fully, we should provide an installer once those are ported.

## Blocked By

bdcompound-xr3 - Port commands, agents, and skills to OpenCode

## Scope

Create plugins/beads-compound/opencode/install-opencode.sh that:

1. Copies plugin.ts + package.json to .opencode/plugins/beads-compound/
2. Runs bun install
3. Copies hooks/ directory
4. Copies commands/ to .opencode/commands/ (once available)
5. Copies skills/ to .opencode/skills/ (once available)
6. Copies agents/ to .opencode/agents/ (once available)
7. Validates .opencode/ is not a symlink (security)
8. Sets restrictive permissions
9. Provides success message

Also create uninstall-opencode.sh.

## Alternative

Add --opencode flag to main install.sh, but separate script is cleaner and avoids complicating existing 541-line installer.
notes: Superseded by new architecture: installers/install-opencode.sh will be created as part of refactoring (see latest bead). Cleaner to have platform-specific installers in installers/ directory rather than separate install-opencode.sh script.

title: Implement subagent wrapup via tool.execute.after (tool=task)
description:
Implement subagent wrapup in the OpenCode plugin using tool.execute.after filtered to tool="task".

## Research Findings (confirmed via DeepWiki + OpenCode source)

The original assumption that session.idle was the only option was WRONG. OpenCode has a clean path:

### How it works
- tool.execute.after fires with tool="task" when a TaskTool (subagent) completes
- The payload includes:
  - title: task description
  - output: final text output from subagent (may include <task_metadata> block with session ID)
  - metadata.summary: summary of tool calls within the subagent session
  - metadata.sessionID: the subagent session ID
  - metadata.model: model used by subagent
  - attachments: optional FilePart array

### Implementation approach
In plugin.js, add a filter in the tool.execute.after handler:

```javascript
if (input.tool === "task") {
  // This is a subagent completion
  // 1. Check output/metadata.summary for BEAD_ID pattern
  // 2. Check if subagent logged any knowledge comments (bd comments add)
  // 3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge logged, inject reminder
  //    (cannot block like Claude Code SubagentStop, but can log warning)
}
```

### Key differences from Claude Code SubagentStop
1. Cannot "block" completion - tool.execute.after is post-execution, not a gate
2. CAN inspect the full subagent output and tool call summary
3. CAN inject a follow-up system message reminding to log knowledge
4. CAN log a warning comment on the bead automatically

### Behavioral design
Since we cannot block (like subagent-wrapup.sh does with decision: block), the OpenCode version should:
1. Parse output + metadata.summary for BEAD_ID references
2. Check if any bd comments add calls appear in the summary
3. If BEAD_ID found but no knowledge captured:
   - Auto-add a reminder comment to the bead: "WARNING: Subagent completed without logging knowledge"
   - Return output with appended reminder text via the hook output
4. If knowledge WAS captured, no action needed

### Integration with plugin.js
This is an additional filter branch in the existing tool.execute.after handler (already needed for memory-capture). Estimated: ~20 lines added to plugin.js.

## Files to modify
- plugins/beads-compound/opencode/plugin.js (add task tool filter branch)

title: Document chore: commit convention for infra/config changes
description:
## What

Add a knowledge entry and optionally a CLAUDE.md note clarifying when to use chore: vs other conventional commit prefixes, specifically for the infra/config changes that tend to slip through as untyped.

## Context

The CNAME commit this week was untyped: 'add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain' should have been 'chore: add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain'. Low stakes individually, but untyped commits break changelog generation and git log --grep filtering.

The ambiguous cases are:
- DNS/domain changes → chore:
- CI config changes → chore: or ci: (either fine, ci: preferred)
- Dependency bumps → chore:
- Gitignore/gitattributes → chore:
- README-only → docs:
- Script/tool additions → chore: (if not user-facing) or feat: (if user-facing)

## What

Add a FACT entry:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "FACT: Conventional commit type for infra/config-only changes: use chore: for DNS records, CI config, gitignore, gitattributes, dependency bumps, non-user-facing scripts. Use ci: for CI-specific changes (either is fine but ci: is more discoverable). These are the cases most likely to slip through as untyped commits."
```

Optionally add a one-liner to CLAUDE.md global instructions if the untyped pattern recurs.

## Decisions

### Locked
- Entry type: FACT (concrete rule, not a design choice)

### Discretion
- Whether to add to CLAUDE.md (only worth it if this is a recurring mistake)

## Testing
- [ ] Entry searchable via `recall.sh "chore commit"`

## Validation
- [ ] Tags include: git, commit, convention, chore

## Files
- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl
- ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (optional)

## Dependencies
None

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: FACT: Conventional commit type for infrastructure and config-only changes: use chore: for DNS records (CNAME), CI config changes, gitignore/gitattributes, dependency bumps, and non-user-facing scripts/tools. Use ci: for CI-specific changes (either is acceptable, ci: is more discoverable). These are the cases most likely to slip through as untyped commits. Untyped commits break changelog generation and git log --grep filtering. Example: 'add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain' should be 'chore: add CNAME for lavra.dev custom domain'.

title: Fix PATTERN type gap: add guidance to use PATTERN: prefix explicitly
description:
## What

Add a PATTERN knowledge entry (and update CLAUDE.md guidance if appropriate) about when to use PATTERN: type explicitly vs burying patterns in DECISION/LEARNED entries.

## Context

The retro found 0 explicit PATTERN: type entries this week despite 7 entries auto-tagged 'pattern' from content. The tags come from keyword detection in memory-capture.sh, but the entry type stayed 'decision' or 'learned'. This makes it harder to filter patterns specifically and defeats the purpose of having distinct knowledge types.

The six types each have a purpose:
- LEARNED: insight from debugging/surprise behavior — one-time discovery
- DECISION: what was chosen and why — specific choice with rationale
- FACT: hard constraint, version req, environment detail — static truth
- PATTERN: recurring convention or idiom — should be reused everywhere
- INVESTIGATION: root cause analysis
- DEVIATION: auto-fix outside bead scope

When something is worth reusing as a convention (not just recording a one-time choice), it should be PATTERN.

## What

1. Add this as a PATTERN entry itself:

```bash
bd comments add bdcompound-7ji "PATTERN: Use PATTERN: type when capturing something meant to be reused as a convention — not just recording a one-time decision. Signal: if you'd want a future agent to do the same thing by default, it's a PATTERN. If you're recording why you made a specific choice, it's a DECISION. Examples of PATTERN: 'always XML-wrap injected content', 'use grep -iF for safe search'. Examples of DECISION: 'chose PostgreSQL over SQLite for this project', 'dropped filesystem scanning due to security risk'."
```

2. Optionally update the knowledge type descriptions in CLAUDE.md or hooks-system.md rule to add a 'When to use PATTERN vs DECISION' note.

## Decisions

### Locked
- The knowledge entry itself must use PATTERN: type (meta-example)

### Discretion
- Whether to also update CLAUDE.md/hooks-system.md rule (assess if it adds clarity)

## Testing
- [ ] Entry is type 'pattern'
- [ ] `recall.sh "pattern type"` returns this entry

## Validation
- [ ] Entry clearly distinguishes PATTERN from DECISION with concrete examples

## Files
- .beads/memory/knowledge.jsonl
- .claude/rules/hooks-system.md (optional — only if adding a clarifying note)

## Dependencies
None — but semantically follows bead 1 and 2 (do those first to practice the pattern)

comments.author: Roberto Mello
comments.text: PATTERN: Use PATTERN: type when capturing something meant to be reused as a convention — not just recording a one-time decision. The clearest signal: can you write it as a rule? 'When doing A, do B' = PATTERN. 'We chose A over B in this case because of C' = DECISION. LEARNED vs PATTERN overlap: LEARNED is for one-time discoveries that surprised you, not rules you would apply again. If you are filing a naming convention or architectural constraint as LEARNED, it is probably a PATTERN. Borderline example from this codebase: 'use beads- prefix for all workflow commands' is a PATTERN (a rule to follow every time). 'Renamed /deepen-plan to /beads-deepen for consistency' is a DECISION (a specific application of that pattern). One PATTERN entry replaces the need for many scattered DECISION entries that all reference the same underlying convention. This entry is itself a meta-example: it describes when to use PATTERN, and is filed as PATTERN so it is recalled when classifying future knowledge entries.
